strengths: Implicit understanding of a real-world use case (employee scheduling/payroll) is demonstrated through code comments
Basic acknowledgment of client needs in variable names and module structure
weaknesses: No explicit scenario description or documented client consultation
No formal rationale explaining choice of this product over alternatives
Absence of any measurable success criteria for later evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the submission lacks a structured Planning section, the student does hint at a practical context (employee timekeeping/payroll). This merits minimal credit in band 1â2 for Planning, yielding a score of 2.

strengths: Sophisticated multi-window PyQt5 application with authentication, role-based interfaces and real-time GUI updates
Comprehensive CRUD operations on SQLite3 with well-organized query functions
Clear encapsulation and separation of concerns across classes, plus effective use of timers, calendar widgets and message dialogs
weaknesses: Citation style in the references section is inconsistent and lacks inline attribution
Discussion of advanced OOP concepts (inheritance, polymorphism) could include deeper code excerpts
Some code comments refer to pseudocode rather than actual implementation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The solution demonstrates high complexity and ingenuity (multi-module design, database integration, dynamic GUI), fully effective use of existing tools, and substantial documentation. This warrants the maximum Development score of 12.

strengths: GUI screenshots reveal some design intent and layout considerations
Manual execution screenshots serve as rudimentary evidence of testing
weaknesses: No formal record of tasks (Gantt chart, time log)
Absence of a systematic test-plan with expected/actual outcomes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides partial design evidence via interface screenshots and ad-hoc tests, placing this in band 3â4 for Solution Overview. The absence of formal logs and plans limits the score to 3.

strengths: All core functions (login, clock-in/out, wage calculation, holiday scheduling) appear to work correctly
Clear, consistent GUI styling and labels support intuitive interaction
Architecture (classes + SQL schema) allows straightforward feature addition
weaknesses: Minor syntax errors and typos in code fragments could hinder direct compilation
Tight coupling of SQL logic in UI code makes some modifications more involved
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The product functions well in line with the video and screenshots, and its modular design supports easy extension. This aligns with band 3â4 for Functionality and Extensibility, earning a score of 4.

strengths: In-code comments demonstrate some reflection on error handling and user feedback
Anecdotal mentions of potential improvements appear in margin notes
weaknesses: No evaluation against explicit success criteria
No documented client or adviser feedback
No concrete future recommendations or next-step planning
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is a limited attempt at reflection, primarily via internal comments. However, the lack of any formal evaluation against criteria or stakeholder feedback places this in band 1â2, meriting a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: A holistic design (data structures, class diagrams, wire-frames) is absent. Screenshots of the finished GUI are shown, but that does not constitute a design overview as defined by the rubric.

There is no dedicated rationale that explains why this specific product was chosen for the clientâs problem or compares alternative approaches. Only functionality is described. Hence the strand falls in the 0 band.

Effective use of existing tools is evident: PyQt5 for GUI, SQLite3 for persistent storage, QTimer, QCalendarWidget, QMessageBox, etc. Tools are integrated coherently and leverage built-in capabilities (stretch headers, validators, joins). This satisfies âappropriate and effective useâ, meriting a score within 5-8; 6 is allotted to reflect good but not expert-level exploitation.
No systematic test-plan table (test number, purpose, expected/actual result) is provided. Consequently the strand earns 0.
The student offers no reflections on future improvements or extensions beyond the existing code. Thus no recommendations are presented, so the strand is awarded 0.

All external information sources are listed in a references section, and key coding techniques (encapsulation, polymorphism, SQL joins) are explained with annotated screenshots. However citation style is inconsistent and code taken from tutorials is not always attributed inline, so the explanation is not fully âcomprehensiveâ. A low score within the 5-8 band (5) is justified.
No record-of-tasks table, Gantt chart, or time log appears anywhere in the submission. Development chronology is therefore undocumented.

The student implements a multi-window PyQt5 application with database creation, CRUD operations, authentication, role-based interfaces, clock-in/out wage calculations and holiday scheduling. This demonstrates multiple interconnected classes, SQL joins, and real-time GUI updatingâclearly above a âlowâ level but not exceptionally advanced algorithms. Mid-upper band (5-8) is appropriate; a midpoint score of 6 best reflects solid but not exceptional ingenuity.
No explicit success-criteria list is provided. Without criteria the project cannot be evaluated later, so the strand scores 0.
There is no transcript, email, or questionnaire showing client/adviser feedback. Score is therefore 0.

The program uses classes, SQL tables and modular methods, so future expansion is possible, yet strong coupling (direct SQL in UI code) would make changes non-trivial. This aligns with âexpansion possible but difficultâ, warranting 2 marks.

The submission contains no section that states a real-world scenario, identifies a client, or documents any form of consultation. All material is purely technical code explanation; therefore the strand meets none of the level-1 descriptors.

Text and screenshots indicate that login, clock-in/out, wage display and holiday scheduling work, but there is no video evidence. Some code fragments contain syntax errors (mis-typed variable names, mixing of pseudo-markup). Hence functionality is likely partial but not fully demonstrated â middle of band 1-2 (score 2).
Because no success criteria were set, no evaluation against them is possible. The strand scores 0.

annotations.comment: There is no rationale explaining why this exact application was chosen over alternatives. A clear Product Rationale section should justify design decisions and discuss trade-offs.
No explicit success criteria are listed against which to evaluate the finished product. Include measurable, client-focused criteria to guide testing and evaluation.
There is no record of tasks, such as a Gantt chart or time log, to demonstrate how development was managed. Add a chronological task record to meet the rubric requirements.
The integration of PyQt5 and SQLite3 demonstrates effective use of existing tools to build GUI and database functionality. This aligns well with rubric expectations.
Creating and initializing the SQLite database via code illustrates moderate system complexity. The student effectively connects GUI actions to persistent storage.
The database query functions are well organized in a dedicated section, showing clear separation of concerns. This supports maintainability and reuse.

The student provides no real-world scenario or client investigation, which are essential for contextualizing the project. Adding a dedicated Planning section with client background and consultation details would strengthen alignment with the rubric.
A holistic design overview (e.g., diagrams of data structures, class models or UI wireframes) is missing. Include high-level design documents to show the planned architecture.
No structured test plan is provided. A table listing test cases, purposes, expected and actual results is needed to demonstrate thorough quality assurance.
The polymorphism explanation is clear, and the runtime connection of buttons to different methods illustrates the concept well.
The GUI screenshots (e.g., login and dialog views) are consistent and professionally styled, supporting intuitive user interaction.
In Figure 8 the code contains syntax errors and inconsistent variable names (e.g., âcursonâ vs âcursorâ), which will prevent compilation. Correct these issues to ensure functionality.
The confirm_details() method tries input validation then database insertion, demonstrating encapsulation of functionality. Good organization of responsibilities in a single class.
The clock-in INSERT operation (Figure 9) cleanly captures real-time data in the logs table. The managerâs ability to view these entries supports system integrity.
The encapsulation discussion is accurate but would benefit from linking to specific code snippets. Consider including annotated examples showing how methods and fields are protected.
The abstraction section nicely describes interface hiding, but adding a specific code excerpt showing the handle_login() method would reinforce the explanation.
Inheritance is described but limited to single-level examples. To demonstrate deeper modularity, consider showing multi-level inheritance or shared base functionality.
Holiday schedules shown in Figure 24 display start and end dates in one cell, reducing readability. Separate columns for each date to improve clarity.
The wage-record view is comprehensive and clearly labels each column, enabling efficient manager review of employee earnings.
The comment-reply table in Figure 23 uses placeholder data and inconsistent spacing. Replace with realistic test data and ensure uniform cell formatting.
The scheduling calendar offers a simple UX but lacks confirmation of remaining holidays. Displaying remaining days and validation errors would enhance usability.
In the Fetch Wage Records code, variable names and arithmetic contain typos (e.g., âhours_wordsâ, â\thetaâ). Correct these to guarantee accurate computation.
The schedule() function contains missing SQL, mismatched variable names, and improper indentation. Fix the query and logic to ensure holidays are recorded correctly.
The Try-Except Block example clearly shows error handling for duplicate usernames, preventing crashes and guiding user feedback.
References are listed but citation formats vary widely and lack consistency. Adhere to a single style (e.g., APA) and include inline citations where appropriate.
There is no evaluation section reflecting on success criteria or client feedback. Include a dedicated evaluation with quantitative and qualitative analysis.
No evidence of client or adviser feedback is included. Collect and document stakeholder responses to validate the product meets user needs.
There are no future recommendations or reflections on how the system could be improved or scaled. Add realistic, well-justified suggestions for next steps.
